summaries.text: Emil Carlebach, a former Blockältester from Frankfurt a.Main, authored a ten-page personal report in German documenting the history of Jewish prisoners at Buchenwald from spring 1938 until 11 April 1945. The account details various Jewish deportation transports between camps, systematic ill-treatment, and mass murder of Jews. Carlebach, who held a responsible position within the Jewish Group of the Antifascist Organisation, further reports on the organised resistance fighters whose efforts succeeded in eliminating antisemitism within the camp and, according to the record, helped save the lives of a considerable number of Jews. The document also references Ernst Heilmann, the former Präsident des preussischen Landtags, and contains further references to the use of dogs, instances of insanity, bribes, and the treatment of Dutch Jews.

The record situates Kurt Eisner junior — identified as appearing on page 5 of the parent document — within a broader testimonial account of Buchenwald beginning in 1938, when approximately 1,000 Jews were brought to the camp as part of a mass arrest campaign. At that time, the camp was reportedly controlled largely by criminal prisoners who cooperated with the SS. The document lists multiple SS officials including Camp-commander Koch, Oberscharführer Sommer, and Lagerarzt Dr. Hoven, alongside references to experiments, children, sabotage, mutiny, and instances of non-Jews helping Jews. Additional locations mentioned include Auschwitz, Camp Gusen near Mauthausen, and the Aussenkommandos at Magdeburg and Zeist.

The source excerpt describes prisoners who were arrested from diverse social backgrounds and age groups, rendering collective resistance difficult. They were housed in an unfurnished cowshed and subsisted on approximately 300 grams of bread and three-quarters of a litre of watery soup per day — rations from which overseeing prisoners reportedly cheated them. Working hours extended to 14–15 hours daily, with sleep limited to approximately five hours per night. The testimony states that three to four prisoners per day were systematically driven to run through the guard cordon in the quarry and shot under the pretext of escape attempts. Apart from a small number who were released, the record implies that all others were killed.

According to the testimony, on both the 23rd and 24th of September 1938, approximately one thousand Jews arrived at Buchenwald on each transport — the camp having by then acquired the status of a liquidation camp. The majority of these men had been arrested following the occupation of Vienna under the same campaign conditions described elsewhere in the document. Among them were some longer-term prisoners interned for anti-fascist views. Over subsequent weeks, imprisoned German anti-fascists reportedly succeeded in purging criminal elements from the overseers' roles, thereby improving conditions for Jewish prisoners to the point that, on 30 January, the Kommandant declared that those overseeing the Jews would henceforth be Jews themselves.

The record attests that Jewish prisoners remained, in the testimony's own words, 'pariahs in the camp' with respect to care, treatment, and working hours, and that the high mortality rate among them must be attributed to these conditions. On 9 November 1938, in the wake of the so-called Rath campaign, over 12,000 Jews entered the camp. The testimony describes SS personnel armed with clubs and whips lining the streets of Weimar, with the result that few Jews arrived at the camp uninjured. The streets were reportedly strewn with bloodied luggage, clothing, and injured individuals — a scene the witness likened to a battlefield.

The twelve thousand newcomers were placed in five emergency barracks, lying up to four deep without organisation, latrines, sanitary facilities, straw mattresses, or blankets. The SS reportedly chased them with whips and pistols, producing such chaos on the first night that it could have been mistaken for a mutiny. Prisoners were selected at random for special reprisals: chained hand to hand in circles and attacked by dogs. Seventy reportedly lost their sanity that night and were initially placed in a wooden shed before being transferred to cells, where they were beaten personally by Oberscharführer Sommer. The record further notes that the presence of well-off business people among the newcomers prompted the SS to exploit the situation for purposes beyond violence.

The testimony describes systematic corruption and extortion by the SS at all ranks. Loudspeaker announcements offered early release to prisoners who surrendered cars or houses to the camp leadership, while daily extortion on pretexts such as 'disturbance, damage, or release expenses' reportedly yielded thousands of Marks. When the campaign ended with a mass release a few weeks later — having cost hundreds of lives — millions in value had allegedly passed through the camp. The corruption and the SS's financial entanglements with criminal prisoners undermined their own authority, which, the record states, contributed substantially to Kommandant Koch's decision to replace criminal overseers with political prisoners in order to reassert command.

At the start of the war in September 1939, the Kommandant formally prohibited medical treatment for Jews, an order which — according to the testimony — was silently ignored in the sick bay. A subsequent mass campaign brought approximately 2,500 Jews to the camp, predominantly stateless men from Vienna and the occupied eastern territories. These prisoners were confined behind barbed wire in five large tents on the parade ground, and their custody was assigned to the notorious SS-Scharführers Blank and Hinkelmann. The group included entire populations of old people's homes and boarding schools, prompting Camp Leader Hüttig to remark, reportedly in the narrator's presence, that it was 'unbelievable that they let this lot run around free up to now.'

The record describes a systematic campaign of murder within the barbed-wire enclosure of the 'little camp': prisoners were killed through forced labour, shot while 'trying to escape,' confined on half rations without underwear, and left standing on the parade ground in winter until approximately sixty died each day. Scharführers Blank, Hinkelmann, and Sommer participated with what the testimony calls 'particular enjoyment,' and containers of poisoned food were reportedly distributed. The narrator attests that antifascist prisoners fought from the outset to halt these measures, operating indirectly against the SS while acting directly against their prisoner collaborators — including successfully replacing the foreman of the work gang supplying food to the little camp.

Comrade Kurt Posener was placed in a position from which he was able, at the risk of his own life, to smuggle extra food to prisoners in the sub-camp. Although the two most dangerous prisoner collaborators, Wolf and Rosenbaum, retained their positions, the resistance managed to install Polish comrade Vulkan as clerk, whose efforts reportedly saved several further lives. Early in 1940, Walter Krämer — a German antifascist in charge of the prisoners' sick bay, later murdered in November 1940 — persuaded the doctor to evacuate the so-called 'hellhole' on public health grounds, claiming it risked spreading infection to the main camp, SS personnel, and surrounding villages. An illegal ambulance service had been set up shortly before, and this rescue action reportedly succeeded in recovering approximately five hundred barely living prisoners.

On 9 November 1939, the day following what the testimony calls 'the provocation of the so-called beer hall assassination attempt,' all Jews at Buchenwald were locked in their barracks in darkness. A group of SS men, including Scharführers Blank and Jännisch, selected 21 young, strong men and took them to the quarry, where they were shot under the pretext of escape. The remaining Jewish prisoners spent three days confined without food in uncertainty. Because the entire camp was subsequently denied food for five days — ostensibly because a pig had been stolen — the consequences were described as especially grave. The record further notes that the earlier evacuation of the 'little camp' had not ended plans for the murder of its former inmates, who were assigned to lethal rock-breaking work details.

The quarry work details, again led by Blank and Hinkelmann, served as the site for what the testimony calls the 'bloody task' of killings alongside routine torments and shootings. The organised resistance against the SS resulted in the denunciation, removal, and murder in the quarry on 3 May 1940 of Rudi Arndt, a Jewish Berlin antifascist who had served as block leader of Block 22. Together with Thuringian comrade Ernst Frommhold, a camp elder who was released, Arndt had been principally responsible for the organised struggle against the SS. Fourteen days later, Comrade Max Vulkan was killed in the same manner. The narrator states he personally witnessed the prisoner-collaborator Wolf threatening Vulkan with death should he ever speak about conditions in the little camp.

In summer 1940, Kommandant Koch, camp leader Schobert, camp doctor Florstadt, and other members of the command staff visited the darning station — a work detail operated by sick inmates — and ordered all Jewish workers there to be transferred to the quarry. Among those shot on their first day in the quarry was the Austrian comrade Hans Kunke. The testimony notes that these targeted actions against Jewish individuals and groups existed alongside a broader pattern of mass death through mistreatment, starvation, and poisoning by camp doctors. Despite all resistance efforts by the anti-fascist groups, the record states, Jews remained consistently the worst-placed group in the camp under SS pressure.

In February 1941, four hundred young Dutch Jews were brought to Buchenwald. Because conditions in the camp — won through sustained antifascist struggle — did not permit the mass liquidation the SS had planned, these prisoners were transferred to Camp Gusen near Mauthausen, where they were reportedly murdered to the last man within eight days. The testimony then documents what it describes as the first example of open resistance to SS murder: Oberscharführer Abraham drowned the Jewish prisoner Hamber in a puddle, and Hamber's brother, formally questioned as an eyewitness, testified truthfully about the cause of death. No other member of the work group dared corroborate his account. When the foreman submitted a list of 28 workers, Oberscharführer Petrik ordered the foreman to add his own name.

The murdered prisoner Hamber's brother, knowing the likely consequences of his testimony, reportedly told the narrator: 'I know that I must die because of what I have said, but perhaps these criminals might hold back a little in future if they are afraid of being reported, and then I will not have died in vain.' Around 9 o'clock he was called to testify again before Kommandant Koch, the Adjutant, the camp leader, and the Rapportführer. The Kommandant reportedly promised him, on his 'word of honour,' that nothing would happen to him if he told the complete truth. The prisoner repeated his earlier testimony and, to general astonishment, was returned to his block half an hour later. Around 11.30 he was called out of the block again — the record breaks off at this point.

The testimony asserts that, at least until 1942, no Jew left the cell block alive regardless of the reason for his detention there. Twenty-nine Jews listed as members of a particular work group were taken to the cell block one by one, and each died on the fourth day after arrival. The testimony describes clumsy attempts to conceal this serial murder — known, the record says, to the entire camp — by transferring each successive victim to a different work detail shortly before his removal to the cells. One prisoner with a broken leg was carried from the sick bay to the cell block on a stretcher on Sommer's orders and met the same fate. Another victim is identified as a short shoemaker named Löwitus.

Jochen Pickard, an antifascist from the Rhineland who had previously been detained at Dachau, was transferred to Buchenwald after completing a penitentiary sentence to which he had confessed — the record suggests — in order to leave the concentration camp system. The SS had not forgotten his years-long refusal to confess to political allegations while in the camp. A few days after his arrival at Buchenwald he was sent to the cell block and did not emerge alive. Ernst Heilmann, the Social Democratic Party President of the Prussian Parliament, was also placed in the cell block in 1940 for, as the testimony states, 'no reason whatever,' and died shortly afterwards.

Kurt Eisner, identified as the son of the former Minister-President of Bavaria of the same name, had according to the testimony already survived several 'extraordinarily coldblooded attempts to murder him.' When questioned as a witness in a minor unpolitical matter, he reportedly told the Lagerführer: 'I have been a prisoner for ten years. You will not expect me to denounce a comrade.' He subsequently died in the cell block. In the winter of 1941, the notorious typhus experiments began, initially using ten Jewish victims, then fifty. After that, the Nazis' own racial ideology reportedly prevented them from using further Jewish subjects, on the grounds that their blood serum should not be injected into so-called Aryans. Early in 1942, around 400 Jews declared unfit for work by Dr Hoven were sent on 'invalid transports' to what proved to be an experimental poison gas station.

Over a year after the transport of approximately 400 Jews to what was revealed to be a poison gas station, the prisoner records office was instructed to register these transport lists as deceased. Hoven reportedly warned comrade Weingärtner of Mannheim, who was then in charge of the camp hospital: 'If anything gets out about this, Weingärtner, we'll both be hanged for it.' The testimony reveals, however, that Weingärtner had in fact falsified the transport lists, thereby rescuing a large number of comrades. The narrator frames the broader resistance strategy as one of protecting the most principled and resistance-disposed comrades where total prevention of SS actions was impossible — an objective he considers vindicated by events on 11 April 1945. The transport itself took place over four days, with three lorries collecting thirty named prisoners at each morning parade.

On the final day of the transport, a lorry drove to the hospital and sick prisoners scheduled for transfer were thrown aboard wearing only shirts, without trousers. On 17 October 1942, all Jewish prisoners from camps, penitentiaries, and jails were transferred to the liquidation camp at Auschwitz. The narrator remained behind with 200 comrades retained as skilled armaments workers. Their position reportedly improved for two reasons: the organised antifascist struggle had largely eliminated antisemitism within the camp population, and the SS considered them indispensable as skilled labour, thereby restraining persecution. In summer 1943, a renewed crisis emerged involving Scharführers Schmidt and Greuel, Arbeitsführer Simons, and Rapportführer Hofschulte.
